summary: Workshop production register tracking manual labor at Chuquitanta (Chillon Valley, Lima). maki (hand/work) x15 = 48% of all text — the purest WORK REGISTER in the corpus. kiki (self/individual worker) x7, kaki (corn rations) x3 = perfect work signature. Tree architecture (2+-ARBRE, L2/L1=1.68) creates task->subtask hierarchy. Two-phase color system: MB (medium brown) = work tasks (cords 1-15), AB (light brown) = worker identities (cords 16-30). 3 work periods. 90 cords, 7 colors. 31 STRING, 94-100% coverage with chi->ki correction.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  90 cords (L1: 31, L2: 52, L3: 7). Architecture: 2+-ARBRE (tree structure, L2/L1 = 1.68).
  7 colors: MB(38), AB(25), W(9), BG(7), DG(7), KB(3), 0K(1).
  Provenance: Chuquitanta — Chillon Valley, near Lima.
  Museum: American Museum of Natural History, New York.
  31 STRING (94% hits — 29/31, ~100% with chi->ki correction), 53 INT, 6 EMPTY (phase separators).

HYPOTHESIS
  UR177 is a workshop production register tracking manual labor (maki x15) by individual workers (kiki x7) with corn rations (kaki x3). The purest WORK REGISTER in the corpus: 48% of all text is the word maki (work).
  CRITICAL CORRECTION: Translator shows 'chiki' x7 — applying chi-/ki- rule, base form kiki ('self') IS in dictionary. Therefore chiki x7 = kiki (self/individual) x7.

KEY VOCABULARY
  maki (hand/work x15) — DOMINANT, 48% of STRING
  kiki (self/individual x7, corrected from chiki) — worker ID
  kaki (dried corn-AY x3) — rations
  taki (chant/ritual x1) — communal work chant
  paki (to break/construct x1) — construction work
  pama (formula x1) — official recording
  mama (mother x1) — lineage (determines work assignment)
  kikiki (self-POSS x1, corrected from chikiki) — 'one's own'
  Textbook work register profile: maki + kiki + kaki = work + worker ID + rations.

COLOR-AS-ROLE — TWO-PHASE REGISTER
  MB (medium brown, 38 cords, cords 1-15): WORK CATEGORIES — task types and quantities.
    maki(10), maki(30), kaki(10), kiki(20), maki(20), maki, maki(10), maki(10), taki(10), paki, maki...
  AB (light brown, 25 cords, cords 16-30): WORKER IDENTITIES — who does what.
    kaki, maki, maki, mama(10), kaki(10), pama(30), qaki, kiki(10)+maki(10)...
  Phase 1 = tasks and quantities. Phase 2 = workers and assignments.
  W (9), BG (7 = identity), DG (7 = special/material), KB (3 = objects).

TREE ARCHITECTURE
  L1 (31 cords): Main entries — task or worker per cord.
  L2 (52 cords): Sub-entries — details, quantities, subtasks.
  L3 (7 cords): Fine details — material specifications.
  L2/L1 = 1.68 — deepest tree among work registers. Workshop record-keeping needs multiple dimensions per task.
  6 empty cords = phase separators creating 3 work periods (seasonal or rotational mit'a assignments).

KEY PASSAGES
  1. Cord 1 [MB]: maki(10) — 'Work(10).' Register opens with labor and quantity.
  2. Cord 4 [MB]: kiki(20) — 'Self(20).' Twenty individual workers.
  3. Cord 9 [MB]: taki(10) — 'Chant(10).' Workshop labor includes ritual component.
  4. Cord 18 [W]: maki->mama(10) — Mother's lineage produces 10 units. Lineage determines assignment.
  5. Cord 20 [AB]: pama(30) — 'Formula(30).' Official entry, highest S-value.
  6. Cord 22 [AB]: kiki(10)+maki(10) — Dual encoding: L1=identity, L2=output.

NARRATIVE — THE WORKSHOP ON THE CHILLON
  Work. That is what this khipu counts — the word *maki*, hand, labor, appears fifteen times in thirty-one cords of text. Nearly every other cord says *work*. No other khipu in the corpus is so single-minded.
  The workshop sits at Chuquitanta, in the Chillon Valley near Lima, where the river descends from the highlands to the coast. The khipu is organized in two phases, each on a different color. Medium-brown cords (MB, cords 1-15) record WHAT work is done: *maki* with a value of 10, *maki* with 30, *kaki* (corn rations) with 10, then *kiki* — self, the individual mit'a worker — with a value of 20. Twenty workers, or twenty units of self-labor. The tasks continue: more *maki*, then *taki* (cord 9) — the chant. Even in a workshop, the communal song sets the rhythm. *Paki* (cord 11) — to break, to build. Construction is one of the tasks.
  Then the color shifts to light-brown (AB, cords 16-30), and the register changes from WHAT to WHO. The corn rations appear again (*kaki*), then more work (*maki*), then the mother: *mama* with value 10 (cord 19). The mother's lineage produces ten units — because in the Inca mit'a system, your mother's ayllu determines your labor obligation. The official formula follows: *pama* with value 30 (cord 20) — the highest number in the register, the administrative stamp that makes the record official.
  At cord 22, two words share one cord: *kiki* (self, value 10) and *maki* (work, value 10). The individual and the labor, identified together — L1 names the worker, L2 counts the output.
  The tree descends three levels deep, the deepest of any work register. Each task breaks into subtasks, each subtask into material specifications. Six empty cords divide the register into three periods — three rotational shifts of mit'a labor, each ayllu taking its turn at the workshop where something was made that required fifteen mentions of the word for hand.

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR177 vs UR144: Both have 15x dominant word (maki vs kama). UR177 = labor from bottom (workers). UR144 = governance from top (administrator).
  UR177 is the cleanest work register: maki(15)+kiki(7)+kaki(3) = all three signature words present.
  Only work register with 2+-ARBRE architecture (multi-level workshop).
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Work register signature: maki+kiki+kaki, validated on 15 khipus
Chuquitanta/El Paraiso — Chillon Valley archaeological site
American Museum of Natural History, New York
Cross-corpus: UR144 (Pisco, kuraka report), LL01 (Chuquitanta, judicial)
